topic: Ethics: Ethical Theories (Natural Law, Situation Ethics, Virtue Ethics)
subject: religious-studies

items.q: What does natural law theory base morality on?
items.correct: Human nature and reason — there are objective purposes (telos) built into humans which reason can discover, generating universal moral precepts.
items.misconception: Natural law means whatever happens in nature is morally right.
items.random_wrong: Natural law is the same thing as the laws of physics.
items.why: The 'naturalistic fallacy' confusion — students assume natural law endorses lions eating gazelles or natural disasters. Aquinas's theory concerns human nature and rational ends, not all natural events.

items.q: What is the focus of virtue ethics?
items.correct: On character and habits — becoming the kind of person who acts well, aiming at eudaimonia (flourishing) through practising virtues that lie between extremes.
items.misconception: Virtue ethics tells you which specific actions are right and wrong.
items.random_wrong: Virtue ethics is about following the Ten Commandments.
items.why: Virtue ethics asks 'what kind of person should I be?' rather than 'what should I do?'. Students try to extract action-rules from it, missing its character-based agent-focused nature.

items.q: What is the central principle of situation ethics?
items.correct: The only intrinsically good thing is agape love; rules can be broken if doing so produces the most loving outcome in the situation.
items.misconception: You should do whatever feels right in the moment.
items.random_wrong: Situation ethics says rules should always be followed.
items.why: Situation ethics is principled (love), not just intuition or relativism. Students collapse it into 'whatever feels right' — Fletcher's actual theory is more disciplined than that.

items.q: What is the difference between act and rule utilitarianism?
items.correct: Act utilitarianism judges each individual act by its consequences; rule utilitarianism asks which general rules maximise happiness and judges acts by conformity to those rules.
items.misconception: Act utilitarianism is about actions while rule utilitarianism is about thoughts and intentions.
items.random_wrong: Rule utilitarianism was invented by Kant.
items.why: Both versions judge by consequences — the difference is the unit of assessment (single act vs general rule). Students invent a rules/intentions contrast, missing that rule utilitarianism is still consequentialist.

items.q: How does situation ethics differ from utilitarianism?
items.correct: Both are consequentialist in form, but situation ethics maximises agape love rather than pleasure or happiness, and grounds itself in a Christian faith commitment to love.
items.misconception: Situation ethics and utilitarianism are the same theory because both look at outcomes.
items.random_wrong: Situation ethics tells you to follow the Ten Commandments strictly.
items.why: Fletcher even spoke of a 'calculus of love', but the maximand differs: agape, not utility. Students collapse the two, losing Fletcher's distinctively Christian, person-centred starting point.

items.q: How many factors in Bentham's hedonic calculus?
items.why: It's 7. “10” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
It's Singer. “Mill” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The real figure is 1863 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
